warning: AI-assisted monitoring. Verify against official legislation before product approval, export, label declaration, or customer compliance confirmation.

rules.parameter: Permitted food additives and their conditions of use
rules.limit: Verification required via GSFA Online Database
rules.sourceTitle: Food Additives DB (GSFA Online)
rules.notes: The Codex General Standard for Food Additives (GSFA, Codex STAN 192-1995) sets forth the conditions under which permitted food additives may be used in all foods. The GSFA Online Database provides all adopted provisions, searchable by additive, functional class, and food category. Users are encouraged to consult the Preamble for interpretation.
rules.evidenceQuote: The "Codex General Standard for Food Additives" (GSFA, Codex STAN 192-1995) sets forth the conditions under which permitted food additives may be used in all foods

rules.parameter: Maximum levels for contaminants (e.g., mycotoxins, metals like arsenic, lead, mercury)
rules.limit: Verification required via Codex General Standard for Contaminants and Toxins in Food and Feed (CXS 193-1995) and other relevant codes of practice.
rules.sourceTitle: Contaminants
rules.notes: Codex establishes maximum levels (MLs) for contaminants in food or feed commodities, which are legally permitted to protect human health. The Codex Committee on Contaminants in Food (CCCF) establishes and endorses these levels.
rules.evidenceQuote: The Codex maximum level (ML) for a contaminant in a food or feed commodity is the maximum concentration of that substance recommended by the Codex Alimentarius Commission to be legally permitted in that commodity.

rules.parameter: Prevention and reduction of mycotoxins in spices
rules.limit: Adherence to Code of Practice required
rules.sourceTitle: Contaminants
rules.notes: The Code of Practice for the Prevention and Reduction of Mycotoxins in Spices (CXC 78-2017) provides guidance for minimizing mycotoxin contamination in spices.
rules.evidenceQuote: CXC 78-2017 Code of Practice for the Prevention and Reduction of Mycotoxins in Spices

rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Food Additives
rules.notes: Use SFA's Calculation Tool for Class II Chemical Preservatives when two or more are used.
rules.evidenceQuote: If two or more Class II chemical preservatives are to be used in the food (e.g. sorbic acid or benzoic acid), use the Calculation Tool for Class II Chemical Preservatives to calculate the permitted levels that should be used.

rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Food Additives
rules.notes: Mandatory safety assessment by SFA before use in food sold in Singapore.
rules.evidenceQuote: Food additives must be assessed for safety by SFA before they can be used in food sold in Singapore.

rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Food Additives
rules.notes: All food businesses must comply with regulatory limits on food additives.
rules.evidenceQuote: All food businesses offering food for sale in Singapore must comply with the regulatory limits on food additives.

rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Food Additives
rules.notes: Food products containing non-permitted additives must not be imported or sold.
rules.evidenceQuote: If your food product contains an additive that is not permitted, do not import or sell it.

rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Food Additives
rules.notes: Only food additives assessed and permitted by SFA are allowed.
rules.evidenceQuote: Only food additives that have been assessed by SFA are permitted for use in food products sold in Singapore.

rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Food Additives
rules.notes: Different limits apply based on additive and food type; check SFA's List of Permitted Food Additives or Search tool.
rules.evidenceQuote: Different limits apply, depending on the additive and the type of food products. Download the List of Permitted Food Additives or use the Food Additives Search tool to check if your additives are allowed.

rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Contaminants in Food
rules.notes: Food businesses should be aware of common contaminants and conduct laboratory tests as needed.
rules.evidenceQuote: Food businesses must be aware of the contaminants that are commonly found in their food products and conduct laboratory tests as needed.

rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Contaminants in Food
rules.notes: Contaminants in food sold in Singapore must be within SFA’s regulatory limits.
rules.evidenceQuote: Contaminants (also known as incidental constituents) in food sold in Singapore must be within SFA’s regulatory limits.

rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Contaminants in Food
rules.notes: All food businesses must comply with regulatory limits on contaminants.
rules.evidenceQuote: All food businesses must comply with the regulatory limits on incidental constituents i.e. contaminants in food.

rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Contaminants in Food
rules.notes: Food products with contaminants exceeding regulatory limits must not be imported or sold.
rules.evidenceQuote: If your food or food products contain contaminants or incidental constituents that exceed the regulatory limits, do not import or sell them.

rules.foodCategory: Meat and food derived from meat
rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Contaminants in Food
rules.notes: Import or sale of meat and derived food containing these residues is prohibited.
rules.evidenceQuote: Regulation 33 of the Food Regulations prohibits the import or sale of meat and any food derived from meat which contains residues of the following compounds: diethylstibestrol (3, 4-bis(p-hydroxyphenyl)-3-hexene) hexoestrol (3, 4-bis(p-hydroxyphenyl)-n-hexane) dienoestrol (3, 4-bis(p-hydroxyphenyl)-2, 4-hexadiene)

rules.parameter: Specific contaminants (microbiological, heavy metals, mycotoxins, marine biotoxins, veterinary drug residues, pesticide residues)
rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Contaminants in Food
rules.notes: Check SFA documents for specific limits on various types of contaminants.
rules.evidenceQuote: There are various limits imposed on different types of contaminants or incidental constituents in food. For more details, please refer to the relevant documents below: Microbiological standards... Heavy metals... Mycotoxins and Marine biotoxins... Veterinary drug residues... Pesticide residues... Other incidental constituents

rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Contaminants in Food
rules.notes: Food businesses must comply with SFA's established MRLs for other veterinary drug residues, available on the SFA website.
rules.evidenceQuote: SFA has also assessed the risk of the presence of other veterinary drug residues in various food commodities, and has established a list of maximum residue limits (MRLs) for veterinary drug residues for compliance by food businesses with effect from 1 May 2020. These MRLs can be downloaded from the SFA website at the following weblink.

rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Food Additives
rules.notes: All ingredients and additives in prepacked foods must be declared on labels.
rules.evidenceQuote: All ingredients and additives used in food products must be declared in list of ingredients on food labels. Your food or food product must also comply with the labelling requirements.

rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Contaminants in Food
rules.notes: Ready-to-eat (RTE) food are not required to be tested for total plate count (TPC).
rules.evidenceQuote: Ready-to-eat (RTE) food are not required to be tested for total plate count (TPC).

rules.sourceTitle: Food Additive Listings | FDA
rules.notes: Specific approved food and color additives and their conditions of use are listed in FDA regulations and inventories.
rules.evidenceQuote: Approved Food and Color Additives and Petitions Under Review

rules.sourceTitle: Substances Added to Food (formerly EAFUS) | FDA
rules.notes: Delisted color additives are listed in FDA regulations (21 CFR 81.10 and/or 81.30).
rules.evidenceQuote: Delisted color additives in FDA regulations (21 CFR 81.10 and/or 81.30) (labeled as "DELISTED").

rules.limit: Must be listed in FDA regulations
rules.sourceTitle: Substances Added to Food (formerly EAFUS) | FDA
rules.notes: Food additives and color additives must be listed in FDA regulations (21 CFR Parts 172, 173 and Parts 73, 74 respectively).
rules.evidenceQuote: Food additives and color additives that are listed in FDA regulations (21 CFR Parts 172, 173 and Parts 73, 74 respectively)

rules.limit: Must be listed in FDA regulations
rules.sourceTitle: Substances Added to Food (formerly EAFUS) | FDA
rules.notes: GRAS substances must be listed in FDA regulations (21 CFR Parts 182 and 184).
rules.evidenceQuote: Generally Recognized as Safe ("GRAS") substances that are listed in FDA regulations (21 CFR Parts 182 and 184).

rules.limit: Must be approved for specific uses in foods prior to September 6, 1958
rules.sourceTitle: Substances Added to Food (formerly EAFUS) | FDA
rules.notes: Prior-sanctioned substances are listed in 21 CFR Part 181.
rules.evidenceQuote: Substances approved for specific uses in foods prior to September 6, 1958, known as prior-sanctioned substances (21 CFR Part 181).

rules.sourceTitle: Substances Added to Food (formerly EAFUS) | FDA
rules.notes: Prohibited substances are listed in FDA regulations (21 CFR Part 189).
rules.evidenceQuote: Prohibited substances that are listed in FDA regulations (21 CFR Part 189) as prohibited from use in food

rules.sourceTitle: Food Allergies | FDA
rules.notes: The nine major food allergens are milk, eggs, fish, Crustacean shellfish, tree nuts, peanuts, wheat, soybeans, and sesame.
rules.evidenceQuote: The major food allergens are milk, eggs, fish, Crustacean shellfish, tree nuts, peanuts, wheat, soybeans, and sesame.

rules.sourceTitle: Chemical Contaminants Transparency Tool
rules.notes: This is a Guidance Level.
rules.evidenceQuote: 3-Monochloropropane-1,2-Diol (3-MCPD) Acid-hydrolyzed protein and Asian-style sauces Guidance Level 1 ppm CPG 500.500

rules.sourceTitle: Chemical Contaminants Transparency Tool
rules.notes: This is an Action Level.
rules.evidenceQuote: Aflatoxins, total Foods Action Level 20 ppb CPG 555.400

rules.limit: Verification required via Chemical Contaminants Transparency Tool
rules.sourceTitle: Chemical Contaminants Transparency Tool
rules.notes: Contaminant levels include tolerances, action levels, guidance levels, derived intervention levels, recommended maximum levels, and advisory levels. Tolerances and action levels are defined in 21 CFR parts 109 and 509.
rules.evidenceQuote: The Chemical Contaminants Transparency Tool provides a consolidated list of these levels.

rules.sourceTitle: Food Allergies | FDA
rules.notes: The FDA issued a final rule defining “gluten-free” for food labeling. A separate rule (August 12, 2020) establishes compliance requirements for fermented and hydrolyzed foods bearing the “gluten-free” claim.
rules.evidenceQuote: On August 2, 2013, the FDA issued a final rule defining “gluten-free” for food labeling, which helps consumers, especially those living with celiac disease, be confident that items labeled “gluten-free” meet a defined standard for gluten content.

rules.sourceTitle: Food Allergies | FDA
rules.notes: Such statements are not required by law and can be used to address unavoidable “cross-contact,” only if manufacturers have incorporated good manufacturing processes and taken every precaution to avoid cross-contact. They must be truthful and not misleading.
rules.evidenceQuote: Such statements are not required by law and can be used to address unavoidable “cross-contact,” only if manufacturers have incorporated good manufacturing processes in their facility and have taken every precaution to avoid cross-contact

rules.foodCategory: Human Food (establishments that manufacture, process, pack, or hold)
rules.sourceTitle: Food Allergies | FDA
rules.notes: The CGMP & PC rule (21 CFR part 117) establishes requirements for allergen preventive controls to prevent allergen cross-contact in manufacturing and packaging and to prevent undeclared allergens. Facilities must put written procedures in place.
rules.evidenceQuote: The FDA’s “Current Good Manufacturing Practice, Hazard Analysis, and Risk-Based Preventive Controls for Human Food” rule (CGMP & PC rule, 21 CFR part 117) establishes requirements applicable to establishments that manufacture, process, pack, or hold human food. The CGMP & PC rule includes requirements for allergen preventive controls to prevent allergen cross-contact in manufacturing and packaging and to prevent undeclared allergens.

rules.sourceTitle: Food Allergies | FDA
rules.notes: Identified as allergenic substances that must be declared on the label.
rules.evidenceQuote: Color additives made from cochineal extract and carmine, which are derived from insects, have been identified as allergenic substances that must be declared on the label of all food and cosmetic products.

rules.sourceTitle: Food Allergies | FDA
rules.notes: The food source name must appear in parentheses following the name of the ingredient.
rules.evidenceQuote: In parentheses following the name of the ingredient. Examples: “lecithin (soy),” “flour (wheat),” and “whey (milk)”

rules.parameter: Declaration of specific tree nut, fish, and Crustacean shellfish
rules.sourceTitle: Food Allergies | FDA
rules.notes: The type of tree nut, and the species of fish and Crustacean shellfish must be declared.
rules.evidenceQuote: The law also requires that the type of tree nut (for example, almonds, pecans, walnuts), and the species of fish (for example, bass, flounder, cod) and Crustacean shellfish (for example, crab, lobster, shrimp) to be declared.

rules.sourceTitle: Food Allergies | FDA
rules.notes: Required to be identified on labels due to potential hypersensitivity reactions.
rules.evidenceQuote: The FDA requires all products containing FD&C Yellow No. 5 to identify it on their labels so consumers who are sensitive to the dye can avoid it.

rules.sourceTitle: Food Allergies | FDA
rules.notes: Must be declared on food labels when present at or above this concentration.
rules.evidenceQuote: they must be declared on food labels when present in food and the concentration in the food is ≥10 parts per million total sulfites.

rules.sourceTitle: Thông tư 28/2026/TT-BCT Ban hành Danh mục các mặt hàng nhập khẩu (kèm theo mã số HS) thực hiện kiểm tra nhà nước về an toàn thực phẩm thuộc trách nhiệm quản lý nhà nước của Bộ Công Thương
rules.notes: This regulation requires state inspection for food safety for specific imported goods under the Ministry of Industry and Trade. The full list of goods and HS codes must be verified in the official document.
rules.evidenceQuote: Danh mục các mặt hàng nhập khẩu (kèm theo mã số HS) thực hiện kiểm tra nhà nước về an toàn thực phẩm

rules.sourceTitle: Thông tư 24/2026/TT-BKHCN Quy định lập dự toán, quản lý, sử dụng và quyết toán kinh phí ngân sách nhà nước lĩnh vực khoa học, công nghệ, đổi mới sáng tạo và chuyển đổi số để thi hành Nghị định số 37/2026/NĐ-CP ngày 23 tháng 01 năm 2026 của Chính phủ quy định chi tiết một số điều và biện pháp để tổ chức, hướng dẫn thi hành Luật Chất lượng sản phẩm, hàng hóa
rules.notes: This regulation details the implementation of the Law on Product and Goods Quality, which applies to all products, including food. Specific quality standards need to be verified in the official document.
rules.evidenceQuote: Luật Chất lượng sản phẩm, hàng hóa

rules.sourceTitle: Thông tư 17/2026/TT-BYT Quy định về quy trình kiểm tra chuyên ngành lĩnh vực y tế
rules.notes: This regulation outlines specialized inspection procedures in the health sector, which includes food safety. Specific procedures need to be verified in the official document.
rules.evidenceQuote: Quy định về quy trình kiểm tra chuyên ngành lĩnh vực y tế
